message:
perf(func): add ignored concat_ws benchmark for text-arg hot path

Adds a `#[ignore = "perf-only benchmark"]` test that times
`ConcatWsFunc::invoke` against a 24-text-arg payload over 50,000
iterations × 5 repeats and reports the best-of-N nanosecond budget.

Why it lives in `mod tests` rather than `benches/`:

  - `ConcatWsFunc` is a private type behind the crate's `Function`
    trait. Exposing it in a `criterion` bench would either require
    a `pub use` (polluting the public surface for benchmark-only
    reasons) or duplicating enough of the function-registry test
    scaffolding to make a stable bench harness — neither pays for
    itself for what's effectively a single hot-path measurement.
  - The `#[ignore]` attribute keeps it out of the default
    `cargo test` run so nightly CI doesn't burn 250k extra calls
    per crate compile. Maintainers run it explicitly via
    `cargo test --release perf_concat_ws_text_args -- --ignored
    --nocapture` when they want a current measurement.
  - `--release --nocapture` is implicit in the workflow because
    the result line is printed via `println!`. This is the same
    pattern the rest of `fsqlite-func` uses for its other ignored
    perf tests (`perf_substr_unicode_args` etc.).

The harness uses `Instant::now()` + `best_ns = best_ns.min(...)`
across repeats so warm-cache effects between rounds don't
contaminate the budget. `std::hint::black_box` wraps both the
arg slice (so the compiler can't hoist the construction out of the
loop) and the return value (so the result text isn't dead-code-
eliminated). The result-length read uses an exhaustive match on
`SqliteValue` rather than `if let`, which makes the bench safe
against future variant additions — a new variant would force a
compile error here rather than silently skip the length capture.

The matching pattern is set up to actually produce the text
(payload-comma-payload-comma-…) so the function's join-and-write
path is exercised, not its empty-arg short-circuit.
